summary: disksweep loads undocumented Windows native binary via process.dlopen on import
details: On require('disksweep'), src/index.js executes `process.dlopen(module, '<pkg>/bin/native/parser.node')` inside a swallowed try/catch, loading a 2.9 MB Windows PE binary (bin/native/parser.node, sha256 b1aace6c...) shipped in the tarball. The binary is undocumented: README explicitly markets the package as 'Zero dependencies. Installs instantly; nothing to audit', yet package.json's `files` array ships `bin/native/`. The pure-JS scanner in src/scan.js fully implements the advertised disk-sweep feature, and no exported symbol from the.node module is ever invoked — the binary is loaded purely for its native init side effects. There is no binding.gyp, no source for the binary, no prebuild-install metadata, no platform check, and no version pinning. The combination of (a) silent native-code execution on library import, (b) a documentation/payload mismatch that actively discourages auditing, (c) gratuitous binary unused by the advertised feature, and (d) errors silenced via empty catch is the canonical opaque-native-dropper shape. Any consumer that requires this package on Windows executes attacker-controlled native code with arbitrary capabilities (filesystem, network, persistence) under the host process.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
